--- SOURCE 3: [PDF] Issue Brief: STEM Education ---
URL: https://wearelee.org/sites/default/files/documents/policy_brief_-_stem_education_-_january_2016_0.pdf

SUMMARY:
Currently, there exists an opportunity gap between underrepresented ethnic minority and female students in comparison to White, Asian, and male students in both

FULL CONTENT:
1 This document is intended to provide objective, nonpartisan analysis that is both fair and evidence-based. Leadership for Educational Equity (LEE) does not endorse or support specific policies or policy positions. Source: The Business Roundtable Issue Brief: STEM Education Together, the fields of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics comprise STEM. Typically, STEM also encompasses computer science and other technical fields. Over 13 federal agencies fund STEM education programs, with total funding ranging from $2.8 to $3.4 billion annually in 2012. The expansion of federally funded STEM education programs reflects the growing workforce trends in STEM fields and the U.S. government’s desire to prepare and expand opportunities for students pursuing these careers. This brief explores the challenges and opportunities associated with STEM education in the United States. Preparing Students and Workers to Compete in the Global Economy With projections of 1 million new STEM positions to be added to the workforce by 2020, STEM jobs (e.g. developer, computer programmer, civil engineer, and mechanical engineer) are driving economic growth and filling job vacancies across the country. In general, STEM professionals benefit from high annual salaries, with a median salary of $77,800. They also enjoy strong job security; there are nearly two job postings per qualified STEM applicant, in comparison to four applicants for every non-STEM job. Most of these jobs require quantitative reasoning and computational skills, which are an increasingly important skillset for all students to possess regardless of the career they pursue. However, in today’s globalized economy, the U.S. may lag behind other developed nations in preparing students for STEM careers. Among the 34 developed countries assessed by the Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA), the U.S. ranked 27st in math achievement and 20th in science achievement in 2012. These results have concerned groups who believe that continued American innovation and competitiveness rests within STEM fields. As put by the National Academy of Engineers, “the U.S.’s global competitiveness is directly related to the quality of STEM education.” Yet according to the National Science Board, “the U.S. education system too frequently fails to identify and develop our most talented and motivated students who will become the next generation of innovators.” As students move into the workforce, their deficiency in STEM education follows them. STEM fields have job vacancies, but the U.S. workforce is not currently suited to fill them. While 40,000 students graduate with computer science degrees and enter the workforce annually, there is an estimated 4 million openings in the field. Moreover, of the approximate 200,000 current job openings in the U.S., 60 percent require some STEM knowledge and 42 Source: The Business Roundtable 2 This document is intended to provide objective, nonpartisan analysis that is both fair and evidence-based. Leadership for Educational Equity (LEE) does not endorse or support specific policies or policy positions. percent require advance STEM knowledge. However, only 17 percent of all bachelor degrees are in STEM majors. The STEM Opportunity Gap In addition to concern about the general unpreparedness of American students to excel in STEM fields, there is further unease about the inequitable gaps among which students have the opportunity receive a STEM education. Currently, there exists an opportunity gap between underrepresented ethnic minority and female students in comparison to White, Asian, and male students in both STEM courses and STEM careers. There was at least a 20-point gap between the average test scores of white students and African American and Hispanic students on the 4th and 8th grade National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) math assessment in 2011. Additionally, far fewer underrepresented students are taking STEM courses as evident in the figure below. Similarly, while the number of female students taking math and science courses are similar to males, overall achievement is slightly lower and females are less likely to pursue higher education in STEM fields than men. These trends of low participation translate into a workforce that lacks representation of both females and minority candidates. Further, the “digital divide” between populations that have access to internet and technology and those that do not may exacerbate inequality in STEM education and careers. Recognizing the need to address the STEM opportunity gap, numerous non-profit STEM organizations have sprung up across the U.S., many of which have an emphasis on expanding access to underrepresented minorities and female students. For instance, the Level Playing Field Institute offers a five-week, three-year math and science summer camp at the University of California Berkeley for underrepresented students. They also offer Saturday school prep-courses to bolster math and science achievement before entering the summer program. Both programs are provided free of cost. STEM Resources in Schools Inadequate STEM resources in schools, from rigorous standards and course offerings to classroom materials and teachers, may account for the lack of students taking or excelling in STEM courses. First, there are sparse STEM course offerings as students advance in grade level. Both local and federal governments have recognized a need to make these courses more widely available. However, bringing these courses to schools may not ensure they have a strong curriculum. To date, only four states met comprehensive standards for K-12 engineering curriculum and only 12 states have a defined engineering curriculum. Furthermore, only 14 states adopted more than 50 percent of the recommended standards relating to computer science education. Similar to the Common Core State Standards in math and Percentage of Public and Private High School Graduates taking Calculus, 2009 Source: National Action Council for Minorities in Engineering 3 This document is intended to provide objective, nonpartisan analysis that is both fair and evidence-based. Leadership for Educational Equity (LEE) does not endorse or support specific policies or policy positions. literacy, the Next Generation Science Standards were developed to provide a rigorous guide for STEM education in the U.S. However, states are not obligated to implement these standards. Moreover, there are currently not enough qualified teachers to teach STEM subjects. This teacher shortage is both an issue of quality and quantity. In 2007-2008, roughly 28 percent of all high school math teachers did not major in math, and teachers who taught math were less likely to hold a subject-specific certification. Over 20 years of research reveals that math and science teachers who majored in the subject are more likely to have positive effects on student achievement. In 2014-2015, 45 states reported a shortage of STEM teachers, which further limits schools’ ability to offer a full breadth of STEM courses. In response to the teacher shortage and the growing demand for more STEM workers, President Obama’s 2015 Budget Proposal included a STEM Teacher Pathways program allocating $40 million to prepare over 100,000 effective STEM teacher and a National STEM Master Teacher Corps to provide the nation’s top STEM teachers with additional resources to mentor math and science teachers in their communities. Additionally, the recently passed Every Student Succeeds Act (ESSA), a reauthorization of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act (ESEA), includes a number of provisions to expand STEM education programs. However, these provisions will not be fully enacted until the 2017-2018 school year, and ESSA’s specific guidelines are yet to be formulated. Discussion Questions  Do the course offerings in your district align with areas of job growth?  How can schools and districts partner with outside organizations to promote and deliver STEM education? What might be the benefits and drawbacks to such public-private partnerships?  What steps would you recommend your school or district take to increase the number of underrepresented students in STEM education? What policies could advance equity in STEM?  Has your school or district experienced a STEM teacher shortage? What policies might attract and retain more STEM teachers? Resources  Federal Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) Education 5-Year Strategic Plan, Committee on STEM Education, National Science and Technology Council (2013)  Science, Technology, Engineering, and Math (STEM): Education for Global Leadership, U.S. Department of Education  Science, Technology, Engineering, and Math (STEM) Education: A Primer, Congressional Research Service (2012)

--- SOURCE 4: The Importance of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusivity in STEM Education ---
URL: https://kidsparkeducation.org/blog/the-importance-of-equity-diversity-and-inclusivity-in-stem-education

SUMMARY:
Issues like equity, accessibility, and inclusivity are top of mind for educators in STEM fields, which cover science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.

FULL CONTENT:
![Kid Spark Education](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hubfs/Logo/KiDSpark-Education-Logo-light.png)
![Kid Spark Education](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hubfs/Logo/KiDSpark-Education-Logo-dark.png)

# The Importance of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusivity in STEM Education

![Middle school age Black female student wearing goggle and sitting in front of a microscope. Male student sits nearby.](https://5375473.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hub/5375473/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-1.png?width=1200&height=650&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-1.png)

## Students of all backgrounds deserve a quality STEM education, yet that still remains out of reach at many schools.

It’s no surprise that issues like equity, accessibility, and inclusivity are top of mind for educators in STEM fields, which cover science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. A thorough STEM education can make a world of difference for students, and in order to deliver that, equity in the education system must be established.

The inequities that exist in STEM education have an impact on diversity in STEM careers, suggesting that a lack of a comprehensive STEM curriculum can limit future career options for students. The demand for STEM professionals continues to grow, so this lack of equity can negatively impact the workforce and economy as a whole, not just the students involved. At Kid Spark Education, we believe that the only way to close the STEM opportunity gap is to introduce children to STEM as early as possible and provide sustained STEM learning opportunities year after year.

Let’s dig into the current state of **equity in STEM** and potential remedies for key issues.

![Black male teacher at a desk with a Black student as the student writes something down.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-2.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-2.png)

![Black male teacher at a desk with a Black student as the student writes something down.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-2.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-2.png)

## Why Equity Matters in STEM Education

In a [December 2022 statement](https://www.whitehouse.gov/ostp/news-updates/2022/12/12/equity-and-excellence-a-vision-to-transform-and-enhance-the-u-s-stemm-ecosystem/), the White House and Biden administration noted the issues in STEM education and beyond, saying, “Many students, families, and educators do not receive the holistic support they need to sustain lives of discovery, especially at the K-12 level.”

This statement accurately summarizes how the absence of a STEM education can affect more than just a batch of standardized testing scores, although those are certainly being impacted. If students do not have the foundational comprehension in STEM, they could fall behind in a way that permanently excludes them from STEM-related fields.

[Many students](https://kidsparkeducation.org/sites/default/files/downloads/kidsparkeducationfactsheet-2022_4.pdf) have minimal or no exposure to STEM education, particularly in their early education and among locations with a high proportion of students of color, students from low-income families, and students from rural communities.

STEM has become an integral part of day-to-day life, from the technology we interact with to the jobs we apply to. Inequity in STEM typically affects the most at-risk students and can set them behind for the rest of their lives.

## History of STEM Education Equity

Education has been historically inequitable. On top of that, STEM is a relatively new approach to education, allowing for further tension and wider gaps in inequalities.

The [term “STEM” was coined in 2001](https://www.britannica.com/topic/STEM-education) by the National Science Foundation (NSF), rearranging the previous term “SMET” and essentially launching the development of STEM education in the United States. This reinvigorated approach addressed the [ongoing Digital Revolution](https://www.techopedia.com/definition/23371/digital-revolution) (sometimes called the Third Industrial Revolution), which had started in the 1980s and showed no signs of slowing down.

In that time, STEM has failed to reach students of all backgrounds, with inequity a serious problem for STEM education across the country.

### Diversity in the STEM Workforce

STEM careers and industries flourished in the following decades, but those jobs were not filled by a diverse workforce, suggesting significant barriers of entry for minorities and women. According to the [Pew Research Center](https://www.pewresearch.org/science/2021/04/01/stem-jobs-see-uneven-progress-in-increasing-gender-racial-and-ethnic-diversity/), “The long-term outlook for diversity in the STEM workforce is closely tied to representation in the STEM educational system, particularly across the nation’s colleges and universities.”

If students see themselves as excluded from or bad at STEM subjects at an early age, they may cement that belief in their plans for the future, avoiding potential careers that involve studying or engaging with STEM.

![A group of elementary aged students of diverse backgrounds smile and look at a laptop.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-4.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-4.png)

![A group of elementary aged students of diverse backgrounds smile and look at a laptop.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-4.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-4.png)

[The University of Texas at Arlington](https://academicpartnerships.uta.edu/articles/education/equity-in-stem-education.aspx) expands on this historical context, stating, “This failure is rooted in systemic racism, gender discrimination, classism and other conscious and unconscious biases and stereotypes woven into U.S. educational systems and pedagogy.” Many of the barriers in STEM education are invisible, like so many other sources of inequality.

### Lack of Diverse STEM Educators

Recently the [Smithsonian Magazine](https://www.smithsonianmag.com/blogs/smithsonian-education/2023/01/05/transforming-k-12-stem-education-by-diversifying-the-stem-teacher-workforce/) reported that 79.3% of public school teachers identify as white, yet nearly 50% of public school students are Black. When students do not have educators that look like them and share their backgrounds, they may be less likely to envision themselves as potentially successful in those fields.

Additionally there is a nationwide teacher shortage, made worse by the recent COVID-19 pandemic. [NPR](https://www.npr.org/2023/03/23/1164800932/teacher-shortages-schools-explainer) reported that, according to the limited data available, 45% of public schools have at least one teacher vacancy. Schools that have high numbers of students affected by poverty and minority students are more likely to be affected by these shortages.

## Benefits of Equitable STEM Education

**Why does equity in STEM education even matter?** The STEM opportunity gap makes it hard for students to catch up, and, once students are behind, they’re less likely to embrace STEM and pursue careers in related industries.

At [Kid Spark Education](https://kidsparkeducation.org/who-we-are), we believe that “[b]y giving students of all backgrounds and abilities an equal chance to learn and love STEM, we are nurturing a next generation of successful professionals, bold thinkers, and passionate leaders.” STEM isn’t going anywhere, so we think a proactive approach to addressing these inequities is needed.

Benefits of equity in STEM education include:

By providing high-quality STEM education early and consistently, educators can disrupt the pattern of educational inequity. This equitable education increases **STEM literacy** and builds **STEM identity** to underserved students.

### What is STEM Literacy?

**STEM literacy** is the ability for individuals to understand and communicate about topics related to the subjects of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics in a meaningful way. STEM literate individuals can identify and solve problems in STEM and with STEM methodologies.

### What is STEM Identity?

**STEM identity** is how a person thinks of themselves as a scientist, mathematician, or other STEM thinker and how a person feels empowered in STEM. Students with a strong STEM identity are more likely to feel confident in their studies.

![Two middle school aged male students smile as they work on laptops.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education.png)

![Two middle school aged male students smile as they work on laptops.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education.png)

## Accessibility and STEM

While many diversity initiatives have supported equity based on race or gender, fewer strategies have addressed inequality and accessibility issues for people with disabilities.

According to [*Life Sciences Education*](https://www.lifescied.org/doi/10.1187/cbe.21-01-0017),

“Research laboratories—an icon of the sciences—are often inaccessible, featuring specialized equipment, requiring long work hours, and having nonergonomic workstation designs…  Similarly, an overemphasis on the visual, analytic, and abstract in STEM can marginalize disabled people who would be better able to use visual or audio representations.”

While that article focused on higher education, all of the issues mentioned for universities can also affect students in elementary school, middle school, and high school, often exacerbated by the fact that adolescents and children may not be as practiced in advocating for their accessibility needs.

Examples of accessibility include the most commonly seen solutions like ASL interpretation and wheelchair access, but also includes accessible language, regular breaks, gender-neutral restrooms, and other accommodations.

When a student is faced with an inaccessible learning environment, they may feel less motivated to engage with the subject matter. For a student with disabilities, it can be hard to see a future in a field that does not provide equitable access. Inaccessibility must be addressed from top to bottom in the field of STEM.

![Young Black female student and young white male student sit with goggles on in front of microscopes.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-1.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-1.png)

![Young Black female student and young white male student sit with goggles on in front of microscopes.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-1.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-1.png)

## How to Achieve Equity in STEM Education

In addition to the work of organizations like Kid Spark Education, government leaders and institutions have continued to tout the importance of addressing the inequity in STEM education.

In December 2022, the [White House Office of Science and Technology Policy outlined](https://www.whitehouse.gov/ostp/news-updates/2022/12/12/equity-and-excellence-a-vision-to-transform-and-enhance-the-u-s-stemm-ecosystem/) five action areas for STEM equity and excellence. Those areas are:

Together, these action areas address systemic problems in the fields of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. By addressing the STEM equity issue on a federal level, the White House has made STEM a key political point worthy of mainstream discussion.

Similarly, the [STEM Equity Initiative](https://stemequityinitiative.org/educational-equity) outlines the four following indicators that create an equitable learning environment:

The indicators from the STEM Equity Initiative can be useful when educators and administrators are gauging their own classrooms’ equitability. Educators can ask themselves: Is this inclusive, normalized, relevant, and empowering?

### Utilize Smart Instructional Approaches

Educators can address the issue of equity in their classrooms by being mindful of how they are teaching. While they may not be able to fix societal issues, they can take a few steps to create a safe and welcoming space for students of all backgrounds to succeed.

![Young Black girl student in a bright yellow shirt smiles as she works on a robotic project.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-3.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-3.png)

![Young Black girl student in a bright yellow shirt smiles as she works on a robotic project.](https://kidsparkeducation.org/hs-fs/hubfs/The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-3.png?width=770&height=400&name=The%20Importance%20of%20Equity%20and%20Inclusivity%20in%20STEM%20Education-3.png)

At the [University of Michigan Center for Research on Learning and Teaching](https://crlt.umich.edu/equity-focused-teaching/principles-strategies-resources), researchers outlined the principles of equity-focused teaching. Those principles are:

These principles are meant to disrupt the historical disenfranchisement of students from specific backgrounds, like those who are minorities, have disabilities, or come from lower socioeconomic means.

### Implement Successful STEM Programs with Kid Spark Education

A comprehensive STEM education should never be kept out of reach of students, no matter their background. Kid Spark Education exists to help address that gap, creating a foundational understanding of STEM and creating a lifelong love of the subjects.

To get started, [explore our curriculum library](https://kidsparkeducation.org/curriculum), or [contact our team](https://kidsparkeducation.org/contact) today to learn more about our programs.

--- SOURCE 5: [PDF] Advancing Equity through STEM Education ---
URL: https://maec.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/MAEC_Advancing_Equity_Through_STEM508_email.pdf

SUMMARY:
MAEC established the Center for Education Equity (CEE) to address problems in public schools caused by segregation and inequities.

FULL CONTENT:
A Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education Advancing Equity through STEM Education About MAEC MAEC is an education non-profit dedicated to increasing access to a high-quality education for racially, culturally, linguistically, and economically diverse learners. We work to promote excellence and equity in education to achieve social justice.
About CEE MAEC established the Center for Education Equity (CEE) to address problems in public schools caused by segregation and inequities. As the Region I equity assistance center, CEE works to improve and sustain the systemic capacity of public education to increase outcomes for students regardless of race, gender, religion, and national origin. CEE is funded by the U.S. Department of Education under Title IV of the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
Authorship This guide was developed by the Center for Education Equity at MAEC. The main authors of this guide are Maria del Rosario (Charo) Basterra, M.Ed., Education Consultant; Carolyn Parker, Ed.D., Sr. Professorial Lecturer, School of Education, American University; Susan Shaffer, M.Ed., President, MAEC; and Pamela Mathews, M.Ed., Education Consultant.
Disclaimer MAEC is committed to the sharing of information regarding issues of equity in education. The contents of this guide were developed under a grant from the U.S. Department of Education under the Equity Assistance Centers Program. However, the contents of this guide do not necessarily represent the policy or views of the Department of Education, and you should not assume endorsement by the Department of Education or federal government, generally.
Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Suggested citation: Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. (2023). Advancing Equity through STEM Education. Bethesda, MD. Edition: March 2023 Introduction 1 Part 1 Principles 5 Part 2 Understanding by Design 9 Principle One 11 STEM is a process for building knowledge about our world and universe Principle Two 15 Culturally responsive STEM instruction yields positive results Principle Three 22 Developmentally appropriate STEM instruction enriches the learning process and promotes academic achievement Principle Four 25 STEM knowledge and perspectives are for everyone Principle Five 30 STEM education should address barriers to learning so that all students experience the power of the learning process for improving their lives and the lives of others Part 3 Conclusion 37 Part 4 References 39 Table of Contents 1 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education Why STEM? Science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM) represent the leading sector for jobs and economic growth in the United States through 2027. According to the Education Commission of the States (2020), U.S. STEM jobs will experience thirteen percent growth in the coming decade where all other job sectors combined will grow by nine percent. At issue is the need for a larger STEM-proficient workforce to strengthen our economy and make the United States more competitive internationally. Yet U.S. business leaders report difficulty finding available STEM-proficient workers (Education Commission of the States, 2020). Although the United States has made recent strides in strengthening STEM education, students of color and girls still lag behind. To meet the growing demand for our 21st century workforce, Pre-K-12 educators in the United States must accelerate efforts to use STEM-leaning principles in their teaching. Bridging diversity and gender gaps also requires increased access for all students in the pursuit of education and careers in STEM fields. STEM education is a fundamental tool for improving people’s lives and strengthening their engagement in our complex and increasingly global society. A basic understanding of STEM principles allows students to think critically, understand the world around them, and become contributing, productive members of society. Schooling in general, and STEM education in particular, provides important learning opportunities that develop critical thinking skills and broaden understanding. All young people can benefit substantially from increased access to quality STEM education and careers. Students of color, however, historically have not had access to high quality STEM teachers or education, and continue to be underrepresented in STEM classes and careers (Education Commission of the States, 2020). Teacher training, increased access to materials and technology at urban and rural schools, and a concerted effort to propel girls and students of color toward degrees in STEM – from an early age – will be the benchmarks of our Nation’s success. Educating our students to meet the economic demands of the 21st century will necessitate an expansion of our perceptions of who will represent our future leaders and workers in the STEM fields and will require an unprecedented push to provide access to historically underrepresented groups. Introduction 2 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
The Context for STEM Education Challenges remain to providing equitable education for all students. Students and teachers of color are significantly underrepresented in STEM education. In addition, considerable achievement gaps continue to exist between white students and students of racially, linguistically, and culturally diverse backgrounds. The problems related to equity and STEM can be tracked historically to a Western perception of science and math. Racial and gender stereotypes have permeated current educational and social conditions. The implicit perceptions artificially limiting who can do math and science remain. The education system reflects the inequities that exist in society at large, and also tends to reinforce these inequities (Bowles & Gintis, 2002). While political and economic changes necessary for equity in education may go beyond the purview of individual schools and teachers, there are school-level policies and classroom practices that can positively influence STEM student outcomes. This guide promotes efforts to create more STEM-equitable learning environments and outcomes for all students.
Education and Schooling The effective inclusion of girls and children of color was absent in the design of 19th century public education. Further, for the majority of White Americans, public education was designed primarily for the purpose of training workers for the factory assembly line. As education became more widely accessible, presenting a means for escaping economic hardship and achieving “the American Dream” unfortunately became increasingly stratified. High quality education was generally reserved for those of higher economic or social status, or both. Equity and opportunity in education have expanded significantly since then. For example, the civil rights era of the mid-20th century saw the desegregation of southern schools; in addition to increased college access and more equitable education rights for girls, people with disabilities (through the Rehabilitation Act), Emerging Multilingual Learners (EML, often called “English Learners”), and African Americans. Yet even with civil rights legislation and increased enforcement of related laws; issues of poverty, racism, and sexism that exist in society at large continue to impact educational outcomes.
STEM Just as Western European culture has held a privileged status in American schools, it has also dominated the supposedly value-neutral realms of science, mathematics, technology, and engineering. Like the education system, Western STEM education and career opportunity have a somewhat checkered past, at times both reflecting and reinforcing the inequality of society as a whole. Historically, the STEM disciplines have been dominated by White men. The contributions of women and people of color have been marginalized throughout history. One notable example is in the case of physicist, Lise Meitner, who, with Otto Hahn and their research group, discovered nuclear fission. Only Hahn was awarded a Nobel Prize (in 1944) for the discovery. Less well known is the fact that current STEM research often does not include patterns of thinking and communicating that are common in non-dominant cultures; patterns that could be beneficial to research aimed at benefitting everyone in society. Even in today’s active learning classrooms, students often are not afforded the chance to decide what questions to pursue and how to pursue them: essentially how to be active in their learning. This reinforces the idea that fluency in STEM is something that must be transmitted from others. When enacted, this notion results in the dismissal of ideas, not only for students who bring non-Western cultures and values to the classroom, but for all students who can benefit from more intellectually autonomous experiences in the K-12 classroom. 3 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education Each principle is addressed by a set of key features that expand and describe how to use the principles when designing and delivering instruction. The table below outlines these principles and their corresponding key features. How to Use This Guide This guide provides principles and features that are necessary for improving equity in STEM education. Rather than a stand-alone resource, it is designed as a tool to be used in conjunction with existing STEM curricula to foster learning within our growing racially, culturally, and linguistically diverse populations. The guide consists of five principles that highlight the central elements of our equity approach to STEM: 1. STEM education is a process for building knowledge about our world and universe.
2. Culturally responsive STEM instruction yields positive results.
3. Developmentally appropriate STEM instruction enriches the learning process and promotes academic achievement.
4. STEM knowledge and perspectives are for everyone.
5. STEM education should address barriers to learning so that all students experience the power of the learning process for improving their lives and the lives of others.
PART 1 Principles 6 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Principles This guide is organized to provide educators with information needed to implement our STEM approach in the classroom. Each principle provides a rationale and summary of relevant research findings that support its importance for providing STEM instruction and assessment from an equity perspective. Following each principle, a description of key features and K-12 classroom examples are provided. The activities can be used as is, or adapted for different grade levels. The guide can be used as a trainer-of-trainer model, a tool to be used by teachers in their classrooms, or both.
The foundation of this guide is a problem-based learning (PBL) pedagogy. Using this method teachers present complex and real-world problems to inspire student learning. Rather than simply delivering content through direct instruction, teachers promote guided, student-driven problem-solving through which students develop understanding of curricular concepts and principles. PBL promotes life-long learning through the development of critical thinking skills, communication skills, and problem-solving abilities, while providing opportunities to work in groups to find and evaluate research materials (Duch, et al., 2001). This approach is easily contextualized within all disciplines, particularly the four STEM disciplines. 7 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education The Five Principles of our Equity Approach to STEM with Key Features Principle Key Feature STEM is a process for building knowledge about our world and universe.
1. STEM learning as a process of knowing that helps students become problem solvers Culturally responsive STEM instruction yields positive results.
2. Cultural responsiveness and validation of non-traditional STEM practices 3. Cultural and gender stereotyping mitigation and encouraging effort-based mindsets 4. Cultural and historical barriers to STEM equity and the need to challenge them Developmentally appropriate STEM instruction enriches the learning process and promotes academic achievement.
5. Developmentally appropriate practices to foster cognitive, language, physical, and socio-emotional areas STEM knowledge and perspectives are for everyone.
6. Encouragement of all students’ agency and ownership of STEM 7. Promotion of a classroom climate that supports equity STEM education should address barriers to learning so that all students experience the power of the learning process for improving their lives and the lives of others.
8. Student-centered, inquiry-based instruction that encourages collaboration 9. Use of pedagogy which addresses different learning types, for example the inclusion of arts to encourage STEM learning 10. Supports to apply STEM learning processes to address real problems in students’ lives PART 2 Understanding by Design 10 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Understanding by Design Throughout this guide, we present each classroom-focused strategy and instructional idea using the Understanding by Design (UbD) Framework (Association for Supervision and Curriculum Development [ASCD], 2012). UbD is a deliberative instructional model that supports equitable teaching and learning in all classrooms. The UbD Framework focuses teaching strategies on the “big ideas” important in instruction. When utilizing the UbD Framework, teachers are empowered to think intentionally about planning curriculum without offering a prescriptive plan. UbD supports teachers in long-term backward planning; allowing them to focus first on desired results from instruction, then student assessment evidence, and finally specific learning experiences and instruction. Teachers become “coaches of understanding,” helping students gain meaning from instruction. The UbD Framework focuses on the continual improvement of all students’ learning: It supports equitable teaching and learning through the development of deep and individualized student understanding. Following the Understanding by Design Framework: Stage 1: Identify desired results (essential questions), Stage 2: Determine assessment evidence (assessment of performance indicator), and Stage 3: Plan learning experiences and instruction (learning plan), (Wiggins and McTighe, 2005). Each instructional suggestion will be presented in the following format: • Lesson • Content Areas(s) • Step One: Essential Questions • Step Two: Assessment of Performance Indicator • Step Three: Learning Plan 11 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education The most important aspect to keep in mind while teaching STEM is that it is not actually a collection of facts and skills. STEM is a process that connects disciplines so that students can understand the interconnections between science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. STEM learning is a process of understanding that allows students to design and propose solutions to problems that are contextualized, authentic, and meaningful (Chalfour, 2010). Through the use of the scientific method which exists in multiple forms, important elements can be learned from attempts to gain new knowledge or problem-solve. These include using logic and careful observations of the natural world in addition to brainstorming, using intuition, finding analogies, communicating in groups, and constructing arguments (Cole, 2008). These are all tools that scientists, mathematicians, and engineers use in the service of making sense of a particular question or phenomenon. In 1936, Albert Einstein wrote, “The whole of science is nothing more than a refinement of everyday thinking.” He believed scientists should not confine their critical thinking to their respective fields but should include connections to the real world; they should be philosophers as well as scientists. By gathering relationships between concepts, scientists can organize the world (Einstein, pp. 22-25).
First, STEM is everywhere, flows from everyday thinking, and should include everyone. In daily weather forecasts (science), handheld devices (technology), buildings we live and work in (engineering), money we use to pay for goods and groceries (math), STEM is everywhere and for everyone.
Second, everyday thinking must then be refined in a way that makes sense, so that conclusions can be drawn and decisions made. Effective STEM learning involves constantly making decisions based on evidence. Like STEM professionals, learners have to decide what questions to ask, what data to collect, the best way to collect that data, what the results mean, how the results fit (or do not fit) with other things they think they know, and whether to consult another expert. This approach leads to the scientific method that everyone can internalize and use to understand and influence the world around them. Principle One 01 The whole of science is nothing more than a refinement of everyday thinking.
- Albert Einstein STEM education is a process for building knowledge about our world and universe.
12 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Third, effective STEM learning is a collaborative and collective process. STEM practice is a social endeavor, as experts communicate not only their findings with each other, but also their methods, thoughts, and ideas. There are multiple cycles of evidence-based iteration throughout the process, which are not done in isolation. It must be noted that getting the best answer to any particular question in STEM comes only at the end of this process. Unfortunately, because tests often measure answers only, the process is passed over or deemphasized. Yet it is the process that is the core of STEM thinking. For example, most students will be better served if they know how to design and conduct an investigation than if they know the speed of light or what various types of bears eat.
Fourth, STEM has a positive impact on students’ means of understanding: There is growing recognition that students’ attitudes, beliefs about disciplinary knowledge, and patterns of choosing certain learning approaches over others play an important role in learning. These attitudes, beliefs, and behaviors are often called epistemologies, and they are not always consciously held. Students who are most successful in a discipline understand the nature of the discipline: how its canonical knowledge is constructed, how to monitor and improve their own learning, and how to explore— from disciplinary evidence—the world and their own lives. Many experts believe that improving students’ epistemological views and behaviors is an important goal; others see it primarily as a means to improve conceptual understanding and problem-solving skills. Regardless of the reason, it is clear that these skills are essential for effective learning. Fifth, STEM educators should use students’ background knowledge and ideas about STEM: Addressing students’ views of the nature of STEM and of learning is something that should occur in tandem with the teaching of content and practices. Special attention should be paid to these views (or to behavior that may indicate certain views), both when planning a lesson and when implementing and assessing it. Students bring to the classroom a range of ideas about knowledge and learning that may come from their home cultures, day-to-day lives, or both. For example, they know that sometimes knowledge is constructed logically (“I figured it out”), invented (“I chose my pet’s name”), and communicated from others (“the teacher told me”). These views are not scientifically right or wrong, but are ways of approaching learning that teachers can use to support more in-depth exploration about content and processes of STEM disciplines.
Sixth, STEM educators should encourage students to reflect on their learning process. STEM learning is best accomplished when students have the opportunity to explicitly reflect on the learning process. Shifting instructional practices to better support the STEM process of building knowledge may indeed lead to significant improvement in students’ understanding of STEM concepts, but it is not sufficient. Reflection helps develop greater understanding of the process itself and the ability to apply it in productive ways.
A key aspect of STEM education is the emphasis on the ability to solve problems. When STEM education begins at an early age, students develop important skills (e.g. communication, collaboration, inquiry, problem solving, and flexibility) that form the foundation for their education (Next Generation Science Standards [NGSS] Lead States, 2013).
13 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education STEM learning as a process of knowing that helps students become problem solvers As described earlier, STEM learning is a process that extends beyond STEM disciplines. An example of a pedagogical approach that supports the inquiry nature of STEM learning is a problem-based teaching and learning approach. Problem-based teaching and learning takes place when “students encounter carefully selected, but ill-structured problems before they experience any instruction in the particular focus area” (Bridges and Hallinger, 1992, pp. 5-6). The uniqueness of ill-structured problems is their open-endedness. Rather than formulating a question precisely that channels students to a certain end or solution, ill-structured problems support flexible STEM-focused thinking. Students must identify what they actually consider the problem to be, what solution they may devise, and then reflect on a process by which to get there. The literature often confuses the term problem-based learning with project-based learning, inquiry-based learning, expeditionary learning, and other terms. While they all share some characteristics, the unique feature of problem-based learning includes a flexible process of inquiry rather than in a more constrained discipline-- focused outcome. An ill-structured problem is one that possesses elements that are unknown and requires multiple solution paths. Students guided by personal opinion, beliefs, or both, are required to examine the evidence from multiple perspectives and use it to arrive at a defensible outcome (Kain, 2003). Although, there is no strict protocol for what problem-based learning must look like, in a K-12 classroom an evidence-based problem-solving approach should include the following (Parker et al., 2017): • Identification of an everyday problem that can be explored from multiple disciplinary perspectives.
• Brainstorming of potential solutions with other learners.
• Completion of background research to inform (from existing literature) what is known about the everyday problem or dilemma.
• Development of a model to help “solve” the problem or dilemma.
• Testing of the proposed model.
• Reflection on the results of the proposed model.
• Improvement on the proposed model.
• Further reflection and improvement until the model is optimized. Incorporating problem-based learning into the K-12 setting supports students through an evidence-based approach while connecting necessary, subject-based knowledge that can include science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. An example of a problem-based learning topic suitable for grades K-12 is scarcity of clean, potable water. In the developed world we take for granted the availability of water. In less developed areas of the world, millions of people lack access to clean, safe water. Further, in rural areas, women and girls often bear the burden of walking miles at a time to gather water from streams and ponds—streams and ponds that may be full of water-borne bacteria, making them and their families sick. Water is the foundation of life. And still today, around the world far too many people suffer from water scarcity.
Water scarcity, and possible solutions to this problem, is a topic that can be studied from a myriad of perspectives in the K-12 classroom and can incorporate, not only the STEM subjects, but social studies, English, foreign language, and the arts. A good course to better understand water scarcity and how it can be applied in the K-12 classroom can be found in the work of The Water Project (2007). What follows is a specific example of a middle school classroom lesson written in the UbD framework.
Key Feature One 14 Advancing Equity through STEM Education Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved.
Performance Indicators • Students will explore, through a problem-based learning approach, how they can help various populations around the world enhance the amount of water available to them. • Students will gain knowledge about the science and social issues facing water purification. • Students will collaborate, research, and share findings about filtration systems from around the world.
• The desired outcome is a workable, affordable, and easily maintainable water purification prototype. Step One: Essential Question How can students assist populations around the world to enhance the availability of potable (drinkable) water by designing cost-effective water filters? Step Two: Assessment of Performance Indicators • A research paper of appropriate length about the worldwide scarcity of potable water. • An engineering notebook describing the process of building the water filter prototype.
• A workable, affordable, and easily maintainable water purification prototype.
Step Three: Learning Plan Lesson plans for this investigation are ubiquitous on the Internet. Two suggested lesson plans for the investigation include: • The Water Project: https://thewaterproject.org/resources/lesson-plans/ • NASA: https://www.nasa.gov/offices/education/programs/national/summer/education_resources/ engineering_grades7-9/E_water-filtration.html Both of the suggested lesson plans align with Key Feature One by framing the problem of water scarcity while approaching the problem from multiple perspectives. Both lessons ask students to apply their own knowledge of the world while researching new knowledge. Through both lessons, students collaborate, research, and share findings; developing new solutions to our world’s water scarcity problem.
Lesson: Water Purification System Targeted Grade Band: 6-8 Content Areas: Science, Technology, Social Studies K-12 Classroom Examples 15 Copyright © 2023. Mid-Atlantic Equity Consortium, Inc. All rights reserved. Advancing Equity through STEM Education Across all disciplines, culturally responsive pedagogy is grounded in instruction that relates course content and practices to the culture that each student brings to the classroom (Ladson-Billings, 2014). Historically, the term culturally responsive teaching began with African American students in the United States, but recent research has found it to be an effective educational approach with other groups of students (Dee & Penner, 2017; Matthews & López, 2018). A culturally responsive STEM education approach seeks to combine students’ diverse cultural backgrounds, knowledge, and practices with the world of STEM. This is especially important in elementary school when students are developing basic functional skills such as reading, writing, and mathematics. STEM instruction in the K-12 classroom can be tailored to bridge the world of a student’s culture, the school, and STEM. For example, Dr. Julie Brown (2015), assistant professor at the College of Education, University of Minnesota discusses a “hybrid knowledge” model for culturally responsive teaching she developed to tap into the lived experiences of students and their families. Brown suggests that teachers learn from students and their families as much as imparting new knowledge to students. Brown provides an example of how this concept can be used in the classroom. In Minneapolis, seventh grade science students learned how the immune system protects against microscopic organisms (e.g., bacteria and fungi) which infect the human body and affect oral health. Minneapolis is home to a large population of Somali immigrants. Therefore, this particular lesson was tailored to the Somali students in the classroom. In the Somali culture, oral hygiene does not necessarily involve brushing teeth with a toothbrush or using toothpaste and mouthwash. In the Somali community, practices that date back centuries are used which are the source of great cultural pride. It is common to use a miswak chewing stick five times a day in advance of Islamic prayers. Charcoal is also used as a whitening and cleaning agent. Scientific research documents that the use of the miswak effectively removes plaque and inhibits periodontal disease. The scientific principles behind miswak and charcoal use for oral hygiene can be an interesting exploratory topic for a science lesson. Incorporating cultural scientific knowledge stimulates student learning, enables teachers to develop an improved relationship with the students and families, and reduces the achievement gap in STEM education. As seen in the case with Somali students, a culturally responsive approach to instruction stimulates learning by building new knowledge in a familiar context, which encourages students to further study STEM subjects. Principle Two 02 Culturally responsive STEM instruction yields positive

enriched_points.expanded_content: Existing STEM education policies often fail to address the unique barriers faced by underrepresented groups, leading to significant disparities in access, resources, and academic achievement.
There is an urgent need for comprehensive reforms that address the root causes of inequity in STEM education, ensuring all students have equal opportunities to succeed.
Successful policies include targeted funding for under-resourced schools, culturally responsive teaching practices, and initiatives aimed at increasing diversity among STEM educators.
